serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of stir-fried kailan with beef is approximately 400 g, providing around 520 kcal, 28.8 g protein, 40.4 g fat, and 10 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Kailan with Beef — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Stir Fried Kailan with Beef (F14080049). Secondary nutrients (sugar, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) not reported by HPB; values carried forward from input record with confidence 0.70 and flagged accordingly. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water not available in HPB or secondary sources for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried kailan with beef. GI estimated at approximately 15 based on the dominant ingredient profile: kailan (Chinese broccoli) is a non-starchy leafy vegetable with negligible digestible starch, analogous to other leafy brassica vegetables (GI ~10–20 per University of Sydney GI Database). Beef contributes no digestible carbohydrate. Oyster sauce and soy sauce contribute minimal carbohydrate. Total carbohydrate is 2.5 g/100 g. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (15 × 10 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.5, which is very low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 491 mg per 100 g; a 400 g plate delivers approximately 1964 mg sodium, representing 85% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce when ordering, or limit portion to 150–200 g to keep sodium intake below 1000 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g/100 g) and estimated GI of 15 result in a negligible glycemic load of 1.5 per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried kailan with beef is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate and glycemic load; however, sodium content is high and should be managed by requesting less seasoning sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.0 g/100 g from beef and cooking oil, trans fat 0.13 g/100 g, cholesterol 22.5 mg/100 g, and sodium 491 mg/100 g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk at full-plate portions.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request minimal added oil and sauce, and consider substituting beef with lean chicken breast or tofu to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which can amplify TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory pathways relevant to RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with chicken, tofu, or oily fish to reduce arachidonic acid load while retaining the anti-inflammatory benefit of kailan's vitamin C and fibre.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Kailan with Beef (炒芥兰牛肉) is a classic Singaporean Chinese dish consisting of kailan (Chinese broccoli, Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) stir-fried with thinly sliced beef over high heat with aromatics including garlic, ginger, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and fish sauce. The dish is finished with a drizzle of sesame oil. Kailan contributes a slightly bitter, robust flavour and firm-tender texture, while the beef provides savoury umami depth. The high-heat wok technique produces characteristic wok hei (breath of the wok) aroma. Per 100 g, the dish provides 130 kcal, 7.2 g protein, 10.1 g fat, 2.5 g carbohydrate, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and 491 mg sodium. The very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g/100 g) and estimated glycemic index of 15 make it suitable for glycaemic management, though the high sodium content (491 mg/100 g; ~1964 mg per 400 g plate) is a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. It is commonly served as a side dish or main at hawker centres, coffee shops, and Chinese restaurants across Singapore, and is a staple of zi char (煮炒) cuisine.

Secondary nutrients (sugar, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) carried from input record at confidence 0.70. Overall record confidence is high given the HPB primary source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific stir-fried preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 2. Dietary tag 'vegetarian', 'plant_based', and 'keto' from the input record have been removed as the dish contains beef and fish sauce; 'halal' status is uncertain as beef sourcing and fish sauce halal certification are not confirmed — is_halal set to false pending verification. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the non-starchy leafy vegetable dominant profile; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Portion size of 400 g sourced from input record (grabfood); this is a delivery-platform estimate and should be validated against a measured serving study.
